stories.headline: Should You Keep Your Phone Plugged In Overnight? Sure, If You Want to Kill the Battery
stories.summary: CNET examines the controversial practice of leaving smartphones charging overnight, exploring the potential long-term effects on battery health and whether the convenience outweighs the gradual degradation risks.
stories.original_title: Should You Keep Your Phone Plugged In Overnight? Sure, If You Want to Kill the Battery
stories.context: Your phone runs on a rechargeable battery, which is like a container for electricity that can be filled up and emptied over and over again. Every time you charge and drain this battery, it wears down a tiny bit—think of it like a rubber band that becomes slightly less stretchy after you use it many times. When you leave your phone plugged in overnight, your device reaches 100% pretty quickly, but it keeps getting small amounts of electricity all night long, which creates heat and continuously stresses the battery. Modern phones have some smart features to stop charging when full, but the constant connection to power still gradually ages your battery, meaning it won't hold a charge for as long after a year or two of overnight charging.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because most people charge their phones while they sleep and don't realize they might be hurting the battery long-term. If your battery dies faster, you'll need to replace your phone sooner, which wastes money and creates more electronic trash.
stories.other_side: Modern phones are smart enough to stop charging when they reach full power, so the damage might be smaller than people think. Some say the convenience of waking up to a fully charged phone is worth the tiny amount of battery wear over time.

stories.headline: iOS 26.4.1 Will Automatically Enable This iPhone Security Feature
stories.summary: Apple's latest iOS 26.4.1 update includes an automatic security feature activation that enhances iPhone protection, as revealed in a support document despite the company typically sparse release notes.
stories.original_title: iOS 26.4.1 Will Automatically Enable This iPhone Security Feature
stories.context: When Apple releases new software for the iPhone, called iOS, they usually don't tell customers much about what changed—they might just say "bug fixes and improvements." This frustrating habit leaves users guessing about what's different or what security dangers have been fixed. Technologoy companies often discover security problems on their own or have researchers report them, then create fixes that typically need to be turned on manually by going through settings menus. Apple's new update version 26.4.1 quietly turns on one of these protections automatically, meaning users get the extra safety without having to search through complicated menus or even knowing the threat existed in the first place.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because hackers are always finding new ways to break into iPhones, and regular people might not know they need extra protection. By turning on security features automatically, Apple makes sure everyone stays safe without having to understand complicated settings.
stories.other_side: Some people don't like companies deciding what's best for them and want full control over their phone's settings. Others worry that automatic features might slow down their phone or use up more battery life.

stories.headline: Tesla Driver Alarmed as FSD Takes Him Directly Into the Path of an Oncoming Train
stories.summary: A Tesla driver reports a terrifying incident where the vehicle's Full Self-Driving system allegedly steered directly toward an oncoming train, raising renewed concerns about autonomous vehicle safety and reliability.
stories.original_title: Tesla Driver Alarmed as FSD Takes Him Directly Into the Path of an Oncoming Train
stories.context: Tesla's Full Self-Driving system uses cameras and artificial intelligence to attempt driving a car with little human help, though it's not truly capable of completely replacing a driver. Many car companies have been working for years on vehicles that can see roads, interpret traffic signals, and avoid obstacles, but perfect self-driving technology has proved incredibly difficult to achieve safely. The data these cars learn from comes from real-world driving, which means mistakes and dangerous situations happen as the system improves through experience—similar to how a human driver must learn from making errors. Tesla has faced repeated criticism and investigations for allowing customers to test incomplete autonomous driving features on public roads, creating situations where the car can make dangerous decisions that a human must quickly overtake to prevent crashes.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because self-driving cars are supposed to make travel safer, but this incident shows they can still make dangerous mistakes. If cars can't avoid obvious dangers like trains, people might get hurt or killed before the technology is truly ready for public roads.
stories.other_side: Tesla says these incidents are extremely rare and that overall, their cars have fewer accidents than human drivers. They argue that every mistake helps them learn and improve the system, making it safer over time.

stories.headline: The Best VPNs for Canada in 2026
stories.summary: PCMag evaluates and ranks top VPN services specifically for Canadian users, prioritizing server coverage within Canada for streaming local content and avoiding regional censorship.
stories.original_title: The Best VPNs for Canada in 2026
stories.context: The internet doesn't work the same way everywhere—each country has its own rules about what websites and videos people can access, and services like Netflix show different movies depending on where you live. A VPN, which stands for Virtual Private Network, is a tool that makes your internet connection appear to be happening in a different location by routing your traffic through computers in other places. Canadians face particular challenges because many popular streaming services limit access to content within their borders, and some websites block or slow down connections from certain regions. When a VPN service has lots of servers specifically located in Canada, it helps Canadians stay connected to their local content while also protecting their privacy and giving them access to entertainment from around the world.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because internet companies track what you do online, and the government can limit what websites you can visit in certain countries. Using a good VPN protects your privacy and lets you watch your favorite shows no matter where you are located.
stories.other_side: Some VPNs might actually collect and sell your data instead of protecting it, and free services often make your internet much slower. Plus, some streaming companies block VPNs entirely, so your setup might not work anyway.

stories.headline: The economist who was terrified of AI just found a rare reason for hope
stories.summary: Economist Alex Imas, who previously warned of AI's potential to devastate labor markets and consumer demand, has developed a new theory exploring potential positive outcomes from artificial intelligence integration.
stories.original_title: The economist who was terrified of AI just found a rare reason for hope
stories.context: Artificial Intelligence systems have become incredibly good at doing work that humans used to do, including writing basic computer code, creating images, answering customer questions, and performing many desk jobs. Economists have worried this could be catastrophic—if machines can do jobs cheaper than people, many workers might lose their incomes, and without enough people earning money to buy things, the whole economy could crash. Alex Imas, an expert who studies how money and people interact, had been one of those sounding the alarm about massive unemployment and economic collapse. Now he's exploring a more hopeful possibility: perhaps AI will not just replace workers but also create new kinds of valuable work or make businesses more productive in ways that create new opportunities for people to earn money in jobs we haven't imagined yet.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because many people are scared AI will take their jobs and wreck the economy, but new thinking shows it could actually create better opportunities for everyone. If experts can find ways AI helps instead of hurts, we might see a future with more wealth and less struggling.
stories.other_side: Some experts say this new optimism ignores the reality that companies will replace humans with AI to save money, regardless of any theories about positive outcomes. They worry that even if things get better overall, regular workers will still suffer in the process.

stories.headline: How the Pebble Index 01 Ring Streamlines Your Daily Note-Taking
stories.summary: The Pebble Index 01 ring introduces a privacy-focused wearable device for capturing ideas through a manually-activated microphone, offering a simplified approach to audio recording without continuous surveillance.
stories.original_title: How the Pebble Index 01 Ring Streamlines Your Daily Note-Taking
stories.context: Voice assistants like Siri and Alexa are always listening—waiting for you to say their "wake word" so they can respond, which means they're constantly collecting sound from your environment. Smart devices have raised huge privacy concerns because many people don't realize what recordings are being made, where they're stored, or what companies do with that information when they capture conversations you thought were private. The Pebble Index 01 ring takes a completely different approach: it doesn't listen or record anything until you actually choose to tap the device to start capturing your thoughts or voice notes manually. By removing the constant "always-on" microphone that other smart devices use, this ring lets people save ideas and important moments without the worry that everything else they say throughout the day is also being recorded and potentially analyzed.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because many people worry that smart devices are recording everything they say, even when they don't realize it. This ring only records when you physically tell it to, which gives people a way to capture thoughts without feeling like they're constantly being watched.
stories.other_side: This is just another device you have to remember to carry and keep charged, which might be more hassle than just using your phone. Some people also question why you need a special gadget when your phone can already record voice notes.

stories.headline: Humans far behind as robot breaks record at Beijing race
stories.summary: A humanoid robot competing in a Beijing half marathon set a new world record, demonstrating the significant advances in robotics technology achieved by Chinese manufacturers in recent years.
stories.original_title: Humans far behind as robot breaks record at Beijing race
stories.context: Building robots that can walk on two legs like humans has been one of technology's toughest challenges because walking requires incredible balance, coordination, and quick adjustments to anything unexpected. For a long time, humanoid robots were slow, clumsy, and could barely walk on flat surfaces without falling over—certainly not ready to run races against people. In recent years, engineers have made dramatic breakthroughs using better computers, more sensitive sensors to detect the ground, and improved motors that can respond with lightning speed. When this humanoid robot completed a half marathon in Beijing and broke the previous record, it showed how far robot technology has advanced—demonstrating that machines can now walk or run steadily for long distances on uneven city streets, something that was science fiction not that many years ago.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because it shows robots are getting much better at physical tasks like running, which means they could soon do many jobs that only humans could do before. As robots become more capable, they might completely change how work gets done in factories, delivery services, and beyond.
stories.other_side: Even though the robot broke a record, it's still much slower than human runners and can't handle many real-world situations besides a simple race. Critics say we shouldn't get too excited about fancy robots when they still struggle with basic, everyday tasks.

stories.headline: Tech products share rise in Türkiye–Germany trade
stories.summary: Trade between Türkiye and Germany is increasingly dominated by medium and high-technology products, signaling a shift in the economic relationship between the two nations according to the German-Turkish Chamber of Industry and Commerce.
stories.original_title: Tech products share rise in Türkiye–Germany trade
stories.context: International trade is how countries buy and sell things from each other, and historically, countries would trade whatever natural resources or products they had plenty of. Technology products are complex items that require advanced factories, specialized knowledge, and modern equipment to build—things like computers, medical devices, sophisticated machinery, and telecommunications equipment. For many years, Turkey and Germany traded mostly basic manufactured goods or agricultural products, making more money from simple items than from advanced technology. Recent reports show that's changing dramatically: more of what these two countries now trade to each other involves sophisticated, high-tech products, which signals that Turkey is building more advanced factories and developing the knowledge needed to create complex technology, while Germany benefits from having another trading partner that can make and buy these higher-value goods.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because when countries trade advanced technology products, it creates better-paying jobs and stronger innovation in both places. A tech-focused relationship between Türkiye and Germany could make both countries richer and more competitive in the global economy.
stories.other_side: Focusing too much on technology might hurt traditional industries like farming and small businesses that can't compete with high-tech products. Some people also worry that relying on foreign technology makes their country less independent and more vulnerable to global problems.

stories.headline: AI hit software engineers first. Here's what they want you to know.
stories.summary: Software engineers on the front lines of AI integration reveal how artificial intelligence is rapidly transforming their profession and offer insights into what this disruption means for other industries facing similar technological shifts.
stories.original_title: AI hit software engineers first. Here's what they want you to know.
stories.context: Software engineers build the apps and websites we use every day by writing instructions in special programming languages that computers can understand. Artificial Intelligence has become suddenly incredibly good at this same work—able to write code, find mistakes, and suggest improvements in seconds that once took humans hours or days. This means many software companies now need fewer human programmers to do the same amount of work, which is leaving many engineers worried about their job security while also forcing others to figure out how to work alongside these powerful new tools. The software professionals experiencing this transformation first-hand have important insights: they're learning that AI can be a helpful partnership tool if used wisely, but that human creativity, problem-solving, and understanding what people actually need from technology are still skills AI cannot replace—the lesson they want other industries facing similar waves of automation to understand before it's too late.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because software engineers are the first workers dealing with AI taking over parts of their jobs, and their experiences show what might happen to everyone else soon. If we understand how they're adapting and what strategies work, we can help other workers prepare before AI reaches their industries too.
stories.other_side: Some engineers say the panic is overblown and that AI is just another tool that makes them better at their jobs, not a replacement. Others believe the focus on software workers ignores that many other jobs involving physical labor can't be easily replaced by AI at all.

stories.headline: Restaurants ban diners' phones during meals as no-scroll trend grows: Put it away or else
stories.summary: A growing number of restaurants and bars across the U.S. are implementing policies that restrict or prohibit phone use during meals, with some establishments requiring customers to lock up devices to encourage more engaging, screen-free dining experiences.
stories.original_title: Restaurants ban diners' phones during meals as no-scroll trend grows: Put it away or else
stories.context: Restaurants used to be places where people would focus on conversation and connection with others, but smartphones have increasingly pulled attention away from the table toward screens, social media, and text messages. As more and more diners continued staring at their devices throughout entire meals, restaurant owners and chefs grew frustrated—watching their carefully prepared food get cold while customers were distracted, and seeing conversations between friends and families barely happen. Some establishment owners started asking people politely to put their phones away, and when customers reported enjoying their meals more with this simple change, a broader movement emerged across the country. Now many restaurants are taking stronger action, requiring customers to place phones in special locked containers or leave them at the door, creating places where people can temporarily disconnect from their digital lives and actually enjoy the food and company right in front of them.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because everyone is so addicted to their phones that they're missing out on real conversations and connections with people right in front of them. If restaurants can help people put down their devices, it might strengthen relationships and remind everyone how enjoyable face-to-face interaction can be.
stories.other_side: For some people, checking their phone is important for work emergencies or family situations, so banning devices completely could cause real problems. Others say it's not the restaurant's job to tell adults how to behave, and customers who don't like it will just take their business elsewhere.
